findings.title: Activation data aggregator — gather all startup context into one JSON payload
findings.detail: Determinism confidence: certain. Estimated token savings: 900. Implementation complexity: moderate. Language: python. Could be prepass: yes. Reusable: yes. On every activation Marcus reads config YAML (user_name, communication_language, document_output_language), checks sidecar memory existence, reads index.md, scans style-bible/ and exemplars/ directories, and invokes read-mode-state.py. Each of these is pure file I/O and YAML/markdown parsing. A single 'preflight-activation.py' script could return a JSON blob: resolved config vars, memory sidecar status (exists, files present, last-modified), style-bible file inventory, exemplar file inventory, and current mode state. The LLM currently burns ~900 tokens just discovering and reading these paths before it can compose the greeting.
findings.action: Create 'preflight-activation.py' that reads config.yaml + config.user.yaml, checks sidecar dir, lists style-bible/ and exemplars/ contents, calls read-mode-state internally, and returns unified JSON. Activation instructions become: run script, interpret result, greet.

findings.title: First-run environment discovery — check required directories and files exist
findings.detail: Determinism confidence: certain. Estimated token savings: 500. Implementation complexity: trivial. Language: python. Could be prepass: yes. Reusable: yes. On first run, Marcus manually checks existence of state/config/course_context.yaml, resources/style-bible/, resources/exemplars/, and the sidecar directory. This is pure filesystem existence checking — completely deterministic. The LLM reads each path, reasons about whether it exists, and reports. A script returns the same answer in milliseconds with zero token cost.
findings.action: Add a '--check-environment' flag to the proposed preflight-activation.py (or standalone 'check-environment.py') that returns JSON: {path: exists_bool} for every required path. Init instructions reference the script output instead of asking the LLM to check paths.

findings.title: Memory sidecar scaffolding — create initial directory and template files
findings.detail: Determinism confidence: certain. Estimated token savings: 700. Implementation complexity: trivial. Language: python. Could be prepass: no. Reusable: yes. On first run, Marcus creates four files (index.md, access-boundaries.md, patterns.md, chronology.md) in the sidecar directory. The file contents are either empty or follow a fixed template derived from memory-system.md and init.md. The LLM currently generates these files from scratch each time, spending tokens on boilerplate. A script with embedded templates does this deterministically.
findings.action: Create 'init-sidecar.py' that creates the sidecar directory and populates template files. Accepts --project-root and --agent-name. Prompt says: 'run init-sidecar.py, then ask user for name and course context'.

findings.title: Access boundary path validation — check file operations against allow/deny rules
findings.detail: Determinism confidence: certain. Estimated token savings: 300 per check, cumulative 1500+ per session. Implementation complexity: moderate. Language: python. Could be prepass: no. Reusable: yes. Marcus is instructed to verify every file operation path against read/write/deny zone rules before executing. The rules are static path-prefix matching (allow state/ writes in default, deny .env always, etc.). The LLM re-reads the boundary table and mentally pattern-matches each path every time — pure deterministic work. A 'check-access.py' script takes (path, operation, mode) and returns allowed/denied with the matching rule.
findings.action: Create 'check-access.py' that encodes the allow/deny rules from access-boundaries.md and memory-system.md. Input: path, operation (read/write), mode (default/ad-hoc). Output: {allowed: bool, rule: string, zone: string}. Marcus calls before file ops.

findings.title: Mode-aware write permission lookup — table-driven file writability check
findings.detail: Determinism confidence: certain. Estimated token savings: 150 per check. Implementation complexity: trivial. Language: python. Could be prepass: no. Reusable: yes. The mode-aware write rules table (index.md: full/transient-only, patterns.md: append/readonly, etc.) is a pure lookup the LLM performs by re-reading the table each time. This is a subset of the access boundary validation and should be folded into the same script.
findings.action: Fold into 'check-access.py' — the sidecar file write rules become additional entries in the permission table. Same interface: (file, operation, mode) → allowed/denied.

findings.title: Config variable resolution — parse YAML configs and extract user/language settings
findings.detail: Determinism confidence: certain. Estimated token savings: 350. Implementation complexity: trivial. Language: python. Could be prepass: yes. Reusable: yes. Marcus reads config.yaml and config.user.yaml, extracts three variables (user_name, communication_language, document_output_language), and applies defaults. This is pure YAML key extraction. The LLM reads the full YAML, finds three keys, applies defaults — all deterministic. Note: this could be a subcommand of the preflight-activation script.
findings.action: Include as part of 'preflight-activation.py' config section. If standalone, a 5-line script: load YAML, get keys with defaults, print JSON.

findings.title: Course hierarchy resolver — extract module/lesson/objective tree from YAML
findings.detail: Determinism confidence: certain. Estimated token savings: 400. Implementation complexity: trivial. Language: python. Could be prepass: yes. Reusable: yes. When the user says 'build slides for cardiac assessment,' Marcus reads course_context.yaml and resolves the course→module→lesson→objectives path. The generate-production-plan.py script already loads course context but only uses it during plan generation. A standalone resolver (or flag on the existing script) could return the full hierarchy or resolve a fuzzy module/lesson reference to its exact ID and objectives, saving the LLM from parsing the full YAML.
findings.action: Add '--resolve MODULE [LESSON]' mode to generate-production-plan.py (or create 'resolve-course-context.py') that returns structured JSON: module title, lesson title, learning objectives, asset inventory for that scope.

findings.title: Asset-lesson pairing validator — verify every artifact has instructional context
findings.detail: Determinism confidence: high. Estimated token savings: 400. Implementation complexity: moderate. Language: python. Could be prepass: no. Reusable: yes. Before marking any production step complete, Marcus verifies: asset has parent lesson in course hierarchy, learning objectives documented, assessment alignment explicit. The hierarchy check is deterministic (traverse course_context.yaml). Objective documentation check is deterministic (file exists, section present). Only the assessment alignment judgment is partially interpretive. A script can handle the structural checks and flag missing elements for LLM review.
findings.action: Create 'validate-asset-pairing.py' that takes asset path and lesson ID, checks: (1) lesson exists in course_context.yaml, (2) lesson has learning_objectives list, (3) assessment alignment section exists in asset file. Returns {valid: bool, missing: [...]}.

findings.title: Context envelope assembler — pre-populate specialist handoff data from state and config
findings.detail: Determinism confidence: high. Estimated token savings: 400. Implementation complexity: moderate. Language: python. Could be prepass: yes. Reusable: yes. When delegating to specialists, Marcus constructs a context envelope with: run ID (from SQLite), content type, scope (from course YAML), learning objectives (from course YAML), style bible sections (file matching), exemplar references (file matching). Most fields are deterministic lookups. A script could pre-assemble the envelope JSON from state DB + course context + file inventory, leaving only user constraints and revision feedback for the LLM to add.
findings.action: Create 'assemble-context-envelope.py' taking run_id, content_type, module_id, lesson_id. Pulls: run state from SQLite, objectives from course YAML, lists matching style-bible files, lists matching exemplar files. Returns partial envelope JSON. LLM fills in user constraints and qualitative fields.

findings.title: Checkpoint outcome record validator — ensure structured fields are present in state writes
findings.detail: Determinism confidence: certain. Estimated token savings: 200. Implementation complexity: trivial. Language: python. Could be prepass: no. Reusable: yes. After checkpoint decisions, Marcus records structured data: artifact ID, version, decision, revision count, feedback, time-to-approval. The record structure is fixed. A post-processing script could validate that the state write contains all required fields with correct types before committing, catching LLM omissions.
findings.action: Create 'validate-checkpoint-record.py' that takes a checkpoint record JSON and validates: all required fields present, decision in {approved, revision, rejected}, revision_count is integer, timestamps parseable. Returns {valid: bool, errors: [...]}.

findings.title: Mode switch state updater — write new mode to state with enforcement validation
findings.detail: Determinism confidence: certain. Estimated token savings: 150. Implementation complexity: trivial. Language: python. Could be prepass: no. Reusable: no. On mode switch, Marcus updates mode in state and adjusts routing. read-mode-state.py handles the read side. A complementary 'set-mode-state.py' could handle the write: update SQLite mode field, validate the switch is valid, clear ad-hoc session section in index.md when switching back to default. Currently the LLM manually performs these state mutations.
findings.action: Create 'set-mode-state.py' taking target mode (default/ad-hoc). Updates SQLite, clears ad-hoc section in index.md if switching to default, returns confirmation JSON. Pairs with read-mode-state.py.

assessments.coverage_gaps: Existing scripts cover state reading and plan generation. No scripts exist for activation pre-flight, access boundary enforcement, sidecar initialization, course context resolution, asset-pairing validation, context envelope assembly, or mode switching write-side. The activation sequence is the highest-value target — Marcus currently burns 900+ tokens on pure file I/O and path checking every time it starts up.
assessments.script_quality: Both existing scripts follow good patterns: argparse CLI, JSON output, graceful fallbacks, stderr diagnostics. New scripts should follow the same conventions.

summary.assessment: Marcus is a coordination-heavy orchestrator with significant deterministic overhead in its activation sequence, access boundary enforcement, and specialist handoff preparation. The highest-ROI script is a unified 'preflight-activation.py' that collapses config loading, environment checking, style-bible/exemplar inventory, and sidecar status into a single JSON payload — eliminating ~900 tokens of pure file-system work on every activation. The second priority cluster is access boundary validation ('check-access.py'), which the LLM performs repeatedly throughout a session by re-reading static rules. Together, the top 4 findings (activation aggregator, environment discovery, sidecar scaffolding, access validation) account for ~2900 tokens of deterministic work — roughly 64% of the total identified savings. The existing two scripts are well-designed and cover their domains; the gaps are in the activation path and runtime validation layers.
